summary: Confirm RP2350-PiZero pinout (HDMI, SD, USB host)
description: RESOLVED from the Waveshare schematic + official demo source (docs/RP2350-PiZero-schematic.pdf, .ref/demo). RP2350B, 520KB SRAM, 16MB flash, PSRAM pad UNPOPULATED. HDMI TMDS on GPIO32-39 (D2=32/33, D1=34/35, D0=36/37, CLK=38/39). microSD SPI: SCK=30, MOSI=31, MISO=40, CS=43, card-detect=22, ~12.5MHz. PIO-USB host: D+=28, D-=29. I2C0 SDA=6/SCL=7, UART0 TX=0/RX=1, WS2812 LED=2. Full pinout in README.md.

summary: Decision: libdvi (PIO) DVI, 320x240 fb hardware-doubled to 640x480
description: RESOLVED. HDMI is wired to GPIO32-39, but HSTX is hardwired to GPIO12-19 only, so HSTX CANNOT drive this board's HDMI -- libdvi (Wren6991/PicoDVI, PIO) is mandatory, matching Waveshare's reference demo. Framebuffer = 320x240 RGB565 (~154KB, fits 520KB SRAM); libdvi scans out pixel/line-doubled (DVI_VERTICAL_REPEAT=2) to 640x480p 60Hz. CoCo 256x192 centers in 320x240 -> 512x384 with blank borders on screen. Config = pico_sock_cfg, invert_diffpairs=false, pio_set_gpio_base(pio,16). See README.md.

summary: DEFERRED: reconcile DVI (~252MHz) vs PIO-USB (120/240MHz) system clock
description: DEFERRED to the USB phase (PIZERO-11+). Decision: prove XRoar rendering first by running DVI at its native ~252MHz TMDS clock (close to the AMOLED port's 250MHz, so emulation gets full headroom and no clock compromise). The conflict only matters once USB host is added: libdvi 640x480p60 needs sys ~252MHz, Pico-PIO-USB needs exactly 120/240MHz (252/48 is fractional -> USB jitter). A custom DVI driver would NOT help -- the bit-rate=sysclk coupling is inherent to PIO DVI. When revisited: feed libdvi a 240MHz-friendly 640x480 timing (24MHz pixel clock ~57Hz, monitor-tolerated) so USB is happy, and verify on a real monitor. [IN-PROGRESS 2026-05-28: spike on path 1 -- sysclk 252->240 MHz with a custom 640x480 timing at 24 MHz pixel clock (~57.14 Hz refresh). Unblocks PIZERO-11.] [DONE 2026-05-28: path 1 verified on hardware. sysclk 240 MHz, custom 640x480 timing at 24 MHz pixel clock (~57.14 Hz refresh) -- monitor accepts, BASIC boots normally, emulator still hits 60 fps (decoupled from display refresh via FRAME_PERIOD_US). Unblocks PIZERO-11.]

summary: Fork reusable skeleton from the AMOLED port
description: Copy board-agnostic code from ~/github/coco-rp2350-waveshare-touch-amoled-18: lib/xroar_core, lib/coco_machine, src/coco_boot.cpp/h, AUTORUN.md, deploy.sh. OMIT board-specific: lib/sh8601, lib/qspi_pio, lib/hal (touch/IMU), src/hw_config.c (AMOLED SD pins), and the AMOLED-rotation parts of the blitter. main.cpp written fresh for libdvi. DONE: xroar_core/coco_machine/psram_stub/coco_boot reused unchanged; AMOLED display/QSPI/HAL omitted.

summary: PlatformIO config for RP2350B + earlephilhower + libdvi
description: platformio.ini: RP2350B board id (-DPICO_RP2350B=1), framework=arduino (earlephilhower). Vendor libdvi (Wren6991/PicoDVI, from .ref/demo Arduino/01-DVI) into lib/; lib_deps Pico-PIO-USB + Adafruit TinyUSB, carlk3/no-OS-FatFS-SD. USB Stack = Adafruit TinyUSB. Keep -O2, build_unflags=-Os, lib_ldf_mode=deep+. Arduino.h before XRoar headers (_Bool gotcha). System clock per PIZERO-02b. DONE (build-verified): board=waveshare_rp2350_pizero, -DPICO_PIO_USE_GPIO_BASE=1 for TMDS on GPIO32-39, DVI_VERTICAL_REPEAT=2, DVI_SYMBOLS_PER_WORD=2. Fixed tmds_encode.S assembly by guarding the SDK config includes in dvi_config_defs.h under !__ASSEMBLER__. Compiles+links to firmware.uf2, RAM 31.7%.

summary: libdvi bring-up: 320x240 test pattern on HDMI monitor
description: Port the hello_dvi reference (libdvi, pico_sock_cfg, TMDS on GPIO32-39, gpio_base=16, vreg ~1.2V) to show a static 320x240 RGB565 test pattern scaled to 640x480p60 on a real HDMI monitor. DVI IRQ/scanout on core1. Confirm stable sync and correct RGB565 colors (watch byte order). Baseline before integrating emulation. DONE: verified on hardware 2026-05-27 — colour bars (black/blue/green/cyan/red/magenta/yellow/white) + white border render correctly at 640x480, confirming pin map, TMDS sync, RGB565 byte order (no swap), and 2x doubling.

summary: SRAM RAM-allocation path in coco_machine
description: coco_machine currently leans on PSRAM for the 64KB CoCo RAM. PiZero PSRAM pad is unpopulated; allocate CoCo RAM from main SRAM (static/SRAM malloc) like the AMOLED port did. Verify against the SRAM budget alongside the 154KB framebuffer + libdvi TMDS buffers. DONE: psram_stub 64KB SRAM pool backs the CoCo RAM; verified by successful link + boot (no PSRAM).

summary: New coco_boot_blit_vdg() -> 320x240 centered, no rotation
description: Re-derive the blitter for the 320x240 RGB565 framebuffer: map the nibble-packed VDG 256x192 buffer + palette into a centered 256x192 region (32px L/R, 24px T/B border), no rotation (landscape native). Feed scanlines to the libdvi colour-buffer queue. Re-derive any lookup tables. Simpler than the AMOLED portrait-rotation math. DONE: coco_boot_blit_vdg_pizero verified on hardware — CoCo image centered with black border, correct colors.

summary: SD mount + ROM load on PiZero SD pins
description: Wire no-OS-FatFS-SD to the PiZero microSD SPI pins (SCK=30, MOSI=31, MISO=40, CS=43, CD=22, ~12.5MHz). Mount the card and load /coco/bas12.rom + /coco/extbas11.rom into the 16KB ROM image via the existing coco_boot ROM loader. DONE: SD mounted and ROMs loaded on the PiZero spi1 pins; Color BASIC reached OK.

summary: Milestone: Color BASIC boots to OK on HDMI
description: Integrate emulator loop + blitter + libdvi scanout: XRoar runs, VDG output renders, Color BASIC reaches the OK prompt on the HDMI monitor. Parity with the AMOLED port's Phase 2. DONE 2026-05-27: Color BASIC boots to OK on HDMI at 54fps (cpu 9.3ms, render+blit 9.4ms per frame). Above the 30fps target; ahead of the AMOLED port (~15fps).

summary: Autonomous AUTORUN.TXT demo on HDMI
description: Get the AUTORUN.TXT autotype + disk/cart demo path working on HDMI (parity with AMOLED Phase 3): @DISK/@CART/@DIRECT directives, LOADM .bin direct-load, autotype queue. Confirm a self-running BASIC/demo on the monitor. DONE 2026-05-27: verified on hardware — dsk finder fixed (skips macOS ._* files, attaches real coco.dsk); sample-sd/coco/autorun.txt autotypes a BASIC graphics demo that self-runs on HDMI.

summary: PIO-USB host bring-up: enumerate a USB keyboard
description:
DONE 2026-05-29 on hardware. Pico-PIO-USB host on the PIO-USB port (D+=28 / D-=29) brought up via Adafruit_TinyUSB, host stack on PIO 1 (libdvi owns PIO 0). Three fixes were needed for a clean enumeration + report flow:

(1) Initialize USB host BEFORE multicore_launch_core1 — alarm_pool_create() spins forever if core 1 is already inside libdvi's DMA-IRQ loop. Init while core 1 is idle, then launch libdvi.

(2) Add -DCFG_TUH_HID_EPIN_BUFSIZE=64 / EPOUT_BUFSIZE=64 to build flags so TinyUSB has a place to land incoming HID reports.

(3) Call tuh_hid_set_default_protocol(HID_PROTOCOL_BOOT) BEFORE USBHost.begin() — common wireless keyboard/mouse dongles need to be talked to in boot protocol for reports to flow without report-descriptor parsing. Doing it via set_default (pre-mount) keeps both interfaces mounting cleanly; trying it via set_protocol AFTER mount breaks the second interface.

Verified: USB-A wireless dongle enumerates (VID:PID 05ac:0256), two interfaces mount (idx=0 boot mouse, idx=1 generic), and [usb] HID report lines stream 8-byte boot-keyboard reports as keys are pressed (e.g. 0x2C = space). PIZERO-12 (HID -> DSCAN) picks up from here.

KNOWN CAVEAT: not all USB devices enumerate — modern USB 2.0 high-speed keyboards/mice (e.g. Keychron K2, standalone gaming mice) do not work with PIO-USB's 1.1-only stack. Period-correct simple wired USB keyboards or wireless USB receivers are the compatible class.

summary: Map USB HID keycodes -> CoCo keyboard matrix
description:
DONE 2026-05-29 on hardware. USB HID boot-keyboard reports are translated into CoCo DSCAN codes and injected via the existing coco_machine_press_key()/release_key() matrix hooks. Verified: typing PRINT "HI" + Enter on a USB keyboard via the wireless dongle yields the expected output in Color BASIC on the HDMI display.

Implementation in src/main.cpp: (1) static 256-entry hid_to_dscan[] lookup mapping HID usage codes to CoCo K_* values with 0xFF sentinel for unmapped; (2) hid_keyboard_apply() diffs the current 6-byte HID keycode set against the previous and issues press/release events for transitions, plus shift modifier handling (HID bits 1/5 of byte 0); (3) per-key release in pump_keyboard() so autotype/serial input no longer clears USB-held keys via release_all_keys().

Notes: works around our test dongle mislabeling its keyboard interface as proto=2 (mouse) by treating any 8-byte report as a boot-keyboard report regardless of declared protocol. tuh_hid_set_default_protocol(HID_PROTOCOL_BOOT) (PIZERO-11) keeps incoming reports in the predictable 8-byte format.

summary: USB gamepad/joystick -> CoCo joystick
description: Map a USB HID gamepad to the CoCo joystick axes/buttons through the XRoar joystick path. Stretch goal after keyboard works.

summary: Dual-core split: emu on core0, video/USB/SD on core1
description: core0 = CoCo emulation; core1 = libdvi DVI scanout (IRQ-driven) + SD/USB host servicing. Pico-PIO-USB host already wants to run on core1, and libdvi scanout runs on core1 too -- confirm they coexist (PIO blocks: libdvi on pio0, PIO-USB on another). Depends on the system-clock outcome from PIZERO-02b. Target >=30fps per the README. DONE 2026-05-27: double-buffered (two 320x240 fbs, core1 samples volatile front ptr once/frame, core0 renders back then publishes) — tear-free, locked 60fps, RAM 89%. Note: fully offloading video to core1 is impossible (libdvi owns core1) and unnecessary (core0 uses 11.3/16.76ms).

summary: Performance pass: instrument timing, tune clock/vreg
description: Add per-frame timing instrumentation (CPU/blit/scanout) like the AMOLED port. Tune the system clock and vreg within the PIZERO-02b constraints to hit >=30fps. Compare real-time % against the AMOLED port baseline (~36%). DONE 2026-05-27: hit locked 60fps. Two core-0 wins: (1) blit skips re-clearing the static border each frame (54->58fps), (2) alpha renderer uses a precomputed glyph->packed-word LUT instead of per-pixel put2 RMW, cutting render 6.4ms->0.56ms. Per-frame core0 = cpu 9.4ms + render 0.56ms + blit 1.5ms = 11.5ms (<16.76ms budget).

summary: Experiment: alternate video device with sprites + extended palette (TMS9918A-style)
description: DEFERRED / future exploration. Add an optional alternate video mode that emulates a sprite-capable, higher-color video chip in place of (or alongside) the MC6847 VDG, to explore 'what if the CoCo had a different graphics board'. Target: TMS9918A-class features first (32 hardware sprites, 15-color palette, tile/pattern modes) -- period-correct alternative to the 6847; stretch to V9938/V9958 (256 colors, more sprites, per-line palette). Prior art on RP2350 to mine: PICO9918 PRO (visrealm/pico9918) emulates the TMS9918A on an RP2350 with HDMI out (open source); console PPUs already run on RP2350+DVI (pico-infonesPlus NES PPU, pico-genesisPlus/pico-smsplus Sega VDPs). NOTE: those projects drive HDMI via HSTX (GPIO12-19); our board wires HDMI to GPIO32-39 so HSTX is unavailable -- reuse only the VDP emulation core, keep our libdvi/PIO scanout. Implementation sketch: VRAM array + register file + per-scanline renderer feeding the existing 320x240 RGB565 libdvi framebuffer; branch coco_machine's VDG hookup (lib/coco_machine) to route to an alternate video device. Investigate how XRoar models video devices and whether the alternate chip is bus-addressable like a cart/expansion. References captured in chat 2026-05-27.

summary: Experiment: synth / sound-chip audio device (AMY + AY-3-8910/SID)
description:
DEFERRED / future exploration. STRETCH that DEPENDS on PIZERO-18: it reuses whatever audio output path PIZERO-18 establishes (HDMI data islands or PWM) -- do not start until native CoCo audio output exists. Companion to PIZERO-16 (alternate video): add an optional synth/sound-chip 'sound board' for the CoCo, beyond the native 6-bit DAC + single-bit sound XRoar already emulates. PRIMARY candidate: AMY (shorepine/amy), a fixed-point MCU synth library that runs on RP2350 (Arduino support for ESP32-S3/RP2040/RP2350/Teensy; AMYboard hardware is ESP32-S3 but the engine is portable). AMY gives virtual-analog (Juno-6), FM (DX7 6-op), partial/breakpoint (Alles/Atari AMY), wavetable/PCM sampler, and an 808-style drum machine -- expose a few as an alternate sound device. Discrete-chip alternatives (portable C cores): AY-3-8910/YM2149 (libayemu, emu2149 -- cheap, period-correct), SID 6581/8580 (reSID heavy; lighter reimpls -- aspirational), YM2612/SN76489 (libOPNMIDI, ymfm -- FM, cycle-hungry). RP2350 reference: Datanoise PicoADK v2.

PREREQUISITE DECISIONS (gate the work):
1. Audio output path -- our Waveshare board has no audio DAC and libdvi isn't set up for HDMI audio. Choose PWM audio, I2S to an external DAC, or add HDMI audio islands to libdvi. Decide first (analogous to the PIZERO-02b clock decision for video).
2. CPU/core budget -- core0 ~11.5/16.76ms at 60fps, core1 owned by libdvi scanout (+ future USB host). AMY/AY are affordable, SID/FM are not. Likely generate a fixed sample-block per frame on whichever core has slack.

Implementation sketch: a synth/sound device fed either by a bus-addressable register interface (chip emulation, branch coco_machine like the VDG hookup) or by a higher-level note API (AMY). References captured in chat 2026-05-27.

summary: Investigate why USB host port on PiZero refuses to enumerate (hardware-level)
description: DONE 2026-05-29 — no hardware investigation needed. The board supports USB host correctly. The earlier failures were device-compatibility, not board hardware: PIO-USB is USB 1.1 only, so high-speed USB 2.0 devices (Keychron K2, standalone mouse) do not enumerate. A full-speed wireless USB receiver enumerated and streamed HID reports as documented in PIZERO-11.

summary: USB hot-replug does not re-enumerate after disconnect
description:
ROOT CAUSE FOUND 2026-05-29 (user diagnostic): not a software defect — insufficient VBUS current from the development USB-C port. Symptom went away completely when the board was powered from a battery / USB power-bank instead of the MacBook USB-C. On the MacBook, hot-replug fails because the host port cannot supply the inrush current a freshly-attached USB device needs while the board is also drawing for libdvi + emulation + USB host servicing. Cold-boot with the device already plugged in works because the device draws supplies before peak board load comes up.

The earlier software analysis (RP2350-E9 leak masking unplug + Pico-PIO-USB SM holding J state) was real but secondary — those made the diagnostic harder but were not the operational blocker. Once power is adequate the existing connection-check path enumerates the new device cleanly.

Action: document the power requirement in README/CLAUDE rather than try to fix in software. A future hardware revision (or use of the BAT JST header for an auxiliary supply) would close this completely.

Original references preserved for any future deep dive:
  - RP2350-E9: https://hackaday.com/2024/09/20/raspberry-pi-rp2350-e9-erratum-redefined-as-input-mode-leakage-current/
  - tinyusb #3296: https://github.com/hathach/tinyusb/issues/3296

summary: Native CoCo audio output (6-bit DAC + 1-bit sound)
description:
Make the CoCo actually make sound. XRoar already emulates the 6-bit DAC + single-bit cassette/speaker tap internally, but nothing on this board drives those samples to an output -- the PiZero is currently silent. Filed after the 43b reconciliation (2026-05-31): 43b treats native audio as a first-class deliverable (COCO-7 decision -> COCO-19 implementation, see its docs/audio-decision.md), and that split is the transferable lesson. This issue is the basic, in-scope audio path; PIZERO-17 (synth/sound-chip device) is a separate far-future stretch that DEPENDS on the output path decided here.

GATE (decide first, analogous to PIZERO-02b's clock decision; full feasibility scoping in docs/audio-decision.md):
Choose the audio output sink. PRIMARY candidate: HDMI audio data-island packets emitted by libdvi -- sound over the single existing cable, no extra hardware (needs libdvi data-island support, which it currently lacks; investigate cost vs the 240 MHz / 24 MHz-pixel-clock timing already in use). FALLBACK: PWM to a free GPIO + RC filter or a small Class-D amp (simplest, needs a wire/part). Decide before implementing.

IMPLEMENTATION SKETCH: tap XRoar's sound_module / DAC output, generate a fixed PCM sample-block per frame (CoCo native rate) on whichever core has slack (core0 has ~5ms headroom of the 16.76ms budget; core1 is owned by libdvi scanout), feed the chosen sink. Verify on hardware: audible BASIC SOUND / PLAY / key-click at correct pitch with core0 frame time staying under 16.76ms.
